narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.80—well above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in several ways, including your middle-class family background and plans to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 55.6% chance that you would fail it, tipping the scales just enough to result in a rejection. This prediction hinges on a few key factors from your profile that carried the most weight in the decision. The strongest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your LSAT score of 27, which is notably lower than the average of 36.7 and signaled higher risk to the model despite your other strengths. Counterbalancing that somewhat was your undergraduate GPA, which had a substantial positive pull toward predicting success, reflecting your strong academic foundation. Your Year 3 school decile ranking of 4—below the average of 5.53—added a smaller but still unfavorable nudge toward failure, suggesting some inconsistency in performance later on. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your LSAT pulled you below their stronger average scores, while your GPA aligned more favorably, but not enough to overcome the gap. These top three elements—LSAT most critically, followed by GPA and Year 3 deciles—dominated the model's thinking for your specific case. While your full-time status and family income were neutral or typical, they didn't sway the outcome. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above the 36.7 average, as that single change could dramatically shift future predictions in your favor. Strengthening recent academic deciles through additional coursework or a post-bac program might also help demonstrate upward momentum. Many students in similar situations, including those from underrepresented backgrounds like yours, succeed by targeting these areas and reapplying—persistence pays off.
